title: Access specifiers
instruction:
Three access levels in C++:

- `private` — only the class itself can access
- `protected` — class and derived classes
- `public` — everyone

`class` defaults to `private`, `struct` defaults to `public`. Same thing, different default.
successMessage: Count is 5 — the 6th increment did nothing (enforced by the method). c.count = 99 would be a compile error — private is enforced at compile time, not runtime.

title: Getters and setters with validation
instruction:
Getters return private values. Setters validate before setting.

Pattern: getters are `const` (they don't modify the object). Setters validate and throw or clamp.
hint: void setAge(int age) { if (age < 0 || age > 150) throw invalid_argument("..."); this->age = age; }
successMessage: Setters are the enforcement point. The Person can never have a negative age — it's structurally impossible. const on getters signals they won't modify the object and can be called on const Person references.

title: const member functions
instruction:
`const` at the end of a function signature means it won't modify the object:

```cpp
int getValue() const { return value; }  // const function
```

- Call on `const` objects: only `const` methods are accessible
- Good practice: mark all read-only methods `const`
successMessage: r is const — only const methods can be called on it. r.scale(2) would be a compile error. Mark every method that doesn't modify state as const — it enables const correctness throughout your codebase.

title: friend functions
instruction:
`friend` gives a non-member function access to private members.

Most common use: overloading `<<` for cout output, since the left operand is `ostream`, not your class.
successMessage: friend operator<< enables cout << temperature directly. The friend function sees private celsius. Use friend sparingly — it breaks encapsulation, but for operator<< it's the idiomatic C++ pattern.

title: Build a validated stack
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a `Stack` class (private array or vector internally):
- `private`: data storage, current size
- `push(int value)` — add if not full (max 10 items), throw `overflow_error` if full
- `pop()` — remove and return top, throw `underflow_error` if empty
- `peek() const` — view top without removing
- `isEmpty() const` / `isFull() const` / `size() const`
- `print() const` — print all items
hint: Use vector<int> or int data[10] internally. Keep private int topIndex or vector.size().
successMessage: An encapsulated stack — users can't corrupt it by accessing data directly. Private internals, public interface, validated operations. Chapter 13 done!
